content.problemStatement:
Banknotes in the state of Strangeland don't have any regulated values like 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, etc. In fact, it's possible to see any positive integer on a banknote of Strangeland. Indeed, this isn't the most convenient thing.

Ann is working as a sweet seller at a shop in Strangeland. Every kind of sweets in this shop has its own cost, and sweets of the same kind have the same cost.

Customers in Strangeland are strange. A customer points at some kind of sweets and gives several banknotes to the seller. This means that he wants to buy a positive number of sweets of that kind. He doesn't tell the exact number of sweets he wants to buy. The only thing Ann knows is: **an 'adequate' customer won't give any extra banknotes**. It means that if you throw away any banknote, the resulting amount of money won't be enough to buy the wanted number of sweets.

Ann has to determine the number of sweets the customer wants. Help Ann write a program which determines this number or tells that it's impossible.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and X – the number of banknotes and the cost of one sweet. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, the values of the banknotes.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing -1 if the set of banknotes is inadequate, otherwise output the largest possible number of sweets the customer can buy.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First case: total=27, max sweets=3, but removing banknote 5 leaves 22 which still buys 3 sweets → inadequate → -1. Second: total=12, max=1, removing the only banknote leaves 0 → adequate → 1. Third: total=70, max=7, removing any banknote leaves insufficient money for 7 sweets → adequate → 7.
First case: total=7 < X, cannot buy any sweet → -1. Second case: total=15, X=5, max=3, removing any banknote leaves at most 10 which buys 2 sweets <3 → adequate → 3.
Total=1, X=1, max=1, removing the only banknote leaves 0 which cannot buy 1 sweet → adequate → 1.

hidden.label: inadequate because of a tiny banknote
general case adequate with equal notes

solution.approach: For a test case let total = sum of all banknotes. The maximum number of sweets that can possibly be bought is K = floor(total / X). If K = 0 the answer is -1 because the customer must buy a positive number of sweets. The set of banknotes is adequate exactly when removing any single banknote makes the remaining money insufficient for K sweets, i.e. for every i: floor((total - Ai) / X) < K. This condition can be checked in O(N). If it holds, K is the required answer; otherwise the answer is -1. The algorithm is optimal because it examines each banknote only once.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T·N) overall.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); X = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        total = sum(a)
        K = total // X
        if K == 0:
            out.append('-1')
            continue
        adequate = True
        for v in a:
            if (total - v) // X >= K:
                adequate = False
                break
        out.append(str(K) if adequate else '-1')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
